event: Delilah cutting Samson's hair while he sleeps
symbols: Samson's hair being cut — loss of the Nazirite vow and divine strength
sleeping Samson — vulnerability through misplaced trust
Delilah's composed gaze — calculated betrayal disguised as intimacy
servant with scissors — the Philistine conspiracy enacted through a proxy
rich garments and jewelry — the wealth Delilah received for her treachery
description: A darkly lit scene showing a muscular man (Samson) asleep on a richly embroidered cushion, his head resting heavily. Above him, a beautiful woman (Delilah) with auburn hair adorned with pearls and wearing an elegant dress with red accents looks down with a composed, calculating expression. To the left, a young man in a red garment leans forward, cutting or handling Samson's hair. The scene is tightly composed with dramatic chiaroscuro, concentrating attention on the act of betrayal.
theological_description: The betrayal of Samson by Delilah is one of the Old Testament's most cautionary narratives about the catastrophic consequences of spiritual compromise. Samson, a Nazirite dedicated to God from the womb (Judges 13:5), possessed supernatural strength as long as his vow remained unbroken — symbolized by his uncut hair. Delilah's persistent seduction and Samson's eventual capitulation (Judges 16:16-17) represent the gradual erosion of consecration through worldly entanglement. When his hair is cut, 'the LORD had departed from him' (Judges 16:20) — the most devastating statement in the narrative. This scene warns of the spiritual danger James describes: 'each person is tempted when they are dragged away by their own evil desire and enticed. Then, after desire has conceived, it gives birth to sin; and sin, when it is full-grown, gives birth to death' (James 1:14-15). Samson's fall typologically anticipates every believer's vulnerability when they exchange the power of consecration for the pleasures of compromise. Yet even Samson's story ends in restoration — his hair grows back, and in his final act he calls upon the Lord once more (Judges 16:28-30), testifying that God's mercy extends even to those who have fallen furthest.

scripture_refs.reason: The direct narrative depicted — Delilah learning Samson's secret and having his hair cut, causing the Lord's departure
Samson's Nazirite vow from birth — the razor was never to touch his head
The Philistine lords bribing Delilah to discover the source of Samson's strength
Desire leading to sin leading to death — the theological pattern of Samson's downfall through seduction
The seductive woman whose lips drip honey but whose end is bitter — Delilah as the archetype
